questions.question: Define permutation and prove that nPr = n!/(n-r)!
keyPoints: Definition: Permutation is an arrangement of objects in a specific order where order matters
For selecting r objects from n distinct objects, first position has n choices, second has n-1 choices, third has n-2 choices, continuing until r positions
This equals n!/(n-r)! by factorial definition
State condition: valid when 0 ≤ r ≤ n

questions.question: What is a derangement? Derive the formula for !n (number of derangements)
keyPoints: Derangement is a permutation where no element appears in its original position
Using inclusion-exclusion principle: !n = n! × Σ(-1)k/k! from k=0 to n

questions.question: Explain circular permutation and derive formula (n-1)!
keyPoints: Circular permutation refers to arrangements around a circle where only relative positions matter
Rotations are considered identical in circular arrangements
Fix one object's position to break circular symmetry (anchor method)
Remaining (n-1) objects can be arranged in (n-1)! ways
For necklaces, divide by 2 for reflection symmetry: (n-1)!/2

questions.question: Find the number of permutations of the word 'MATHEMATICS' with all vowels together
keyPoints: Vowels: A,E,A,I (4 letters with A repeated twice)
Treat all vowels as single unit: M,M,T,T,H,C,S + (AEAI block) = 8 items
M repeated twice, T repeated twice: 8!/(2!×2!) = 40320/4 = 10080

questions.question: If 5P_r = 120, find the value of r. Also find n if nP3 = 60
How many 4-digit numbers can be formed using digits 1,2,3,4,5,6 (no repetition) that are divisible by 3?
keyPoints: A number is divisible by 3 if sum of digits is divisible by 3
Groups of 4 digits with sum divisible by 3: {1,2,3,6}=12, {1,2,4,5}=12, {1,3,4,6}=14, {1,5,6,3}=15? Need systematic approach
Actually: List all combinations of 4 digits and count permutations

questions.question: Find the rank of the permutation 2431 in lexicographic order among all permutations of {1,2,3,4}
keyPoints: List permutations in lexicographic order starting from 1234
Starting with 2: fix first digit as 2, remaining are 1,3,4
For 2431: second digit is 4 - permutations starting with 24: remaining 1,3
Wait - we need before 2431: after 24, digits less than 4 in remaining positions
Starting with 2, second digit 4: now third digit 3 is smallest of remaining (1,3), so no permutations before 2431 with prefix 243

questions.question: A committee of 5 is to be formed from 6 men and 5 women. In how many ways can this be done if: (i) there is no restriction (ii) the committee has exactly 3 men (iii) the committee has at least 3 women (iv) a particular man and woman cannot serve together?
keyPoints: (ii) Exactly 3 men: Choose 3 men from 6 (6C3=20) and 2 women from 5 (5C2=10): 20×10=200 ways
(iv) Particular man M1 and woman W1 cannot serve together:
Committees with both M1 and W1: Choose M1, W1 + 3 from remaining 9: C(9,3)=84

questions.question:
(a) Prove that for circular permutations of n objects, the number is (n-1)!
(b) Find the number of ways to seat 8 people around a round table if (i) there is no restriction (ii) two particular persons sit opposite each other (iii) two particular persons always sit together
keyPoints: (a) In circular arrangement, rotations are considered identical. Fix one person's position (break symmetry), arrange remaining (n-1) persons in (n-1)! ways. QED.

(ii) Two opposite persons: Consider them as a pair. They divide circle into 2 halves. Treat them as one unit with internal arrangements (2 ways: A-B or B-A). Now we have 7 units around circle: (7-1)! × 2 = 6! × 2 = 720 × 2 = 1440 ways

(iii) Two particular persons always together: Treat them as one unit. Now we have 7 units to arrange in a circle: (7-1)! = 6! = 720. The pair can be arranged in 2 ways internally. Total = 720 × 2 = 1440 ways

questions.question: Find the number of permutations of 4 men and 3 women around a round table such that: (i) no restrictions (ii) no two women sit together (iii) all women sit together
keyPoints: (i) No restrictions: Total 7 persons, circular arrangement = (7-1)! = 6! = 720 ways

(ii) No two women together: First arrange 4 men in circle: (4-1)! = 3! = 6 ways. This creates 4 gaps between men. Choose 3 gaps for 3 women: 4P3 = 4×3×2 = 24 ways. Arrange women in those gaps: 3! = 6 ways. Total = 6 × 24 × 6 = 864 ways

(iii) All women together: Treat 3 women as one block. Now we have 4 men + 1 block = 5 units. Arrange in circle: (5-1)! = 4! = 24 ways. Women can be arranged internally: 3! = 6 ways. Total = 24 × 6 = 144 ways

questions.question: Derive the formula for derangements (!n) using inclusion-exclusion principle. Hence find !5 and !6.
keyPoints: Let A_i = event that i-th object is in its original position. We want permutations with no A_i occurring.
